workMixDesign.sourceDerivedSummary: For this mid-level game designer profile, the strongest signals are digital tool and artifact work, cognitive design decisions, written specs, and frequent cross-functional communication. O*NET, YouTube transcripts, and alternative public industry sources describe designers/planners creating design documents, flowcharts, proposals, test plans, level blockouts, spreadsheets, playtest loops, prototypes, acceptance criteria, and meeting-heavy coordination with specialists. Physical device/controller use appears, but it is supporting context rather than the core work.

sourceEvidence: A Japanese game planner verifies bugs on physical test phones and uses test devices when simulators are not enough.
A Street Fighter planner checks gameplay using two controllers.
A level designer builds and plays rough blockouts, but the core manipulation is digital/editor work.
physicalProceduralTool.simulatorImplication: Do not create a physical playground; represent the hands-on part through concrete digital design notes and playtest-based priority ranking.

sourceEvidence: O*NET lists design documentation, mock-up screenshots, flowcharts, test plans, and QA specifications.
Practitioner sources mention documents, presentations, spreadsheets, editor work, blockouts, and repeated playtesting.
Alternative sources from The Level Design Book, Riot Games, GitBook, and Game Developer emphasize blockouts, prototypes, designer tools, GDDs, and shared design artifacts.
digitalToolArtifactWork.simulatorImplication: Most active scenes use Notion/doc, playtest note capture, Slack, voice review, and email work surfaces.

sourceEvidence: O*NET emphasizes balancing gameplay, devising challenges, solving problems, and judging quality.
Designer sources emphasize finding fun inside technical, budget, IP, team, and time constraints.
Alternative sources such as The Door Problem and Riot prototype articles frame game design as player-experience problem solving under cross-discipline and scope constraints.
cognitiveAnalysisDecision.simulatorImplication: The player must frame the player problem, define observable playtest signals, prioritize evidence, and defend tradeoffs.

sourceEvidence: O*NET lists formal design documentation, production schedules, prototype goals, communication plans, and test specifications.
A designer describes spending blocks of time on design projects, reviewing content, and providing feedback between meetings.
GitBook and Game Developer public articles describe game design documents as a source of truth and communication artifact for teams.
writtenDocumentationArtifact.simulatorImplication: Require typed hook notes, spec updates, meeting prep, and a live engineering handoff.

sourceEvidence: O*NET lists daily email, face-to-face discussion, and work with teams as important context.
Day-in-the-life videos show planners meeting with level, effects, costume, battle, and project teams.
Riot's agile creative development article emphasizes cross-functional teams, standups, playtest reviews, QA, and acceptance criteria.
spokenInterpersonalCommunication.simulatorImplication: Include a realistic in-person engineering handoff where the student clarifies buildable rules and edge cases.

sourceEvidence: A professional designer describes multiple meetings throughout the day and design work in blocks between meetings.
Game planner sources show movement between meetings, desk work, team play sessions, and bug verification.
Riot's development article reinforces the rhythm of playtests, reviews, and feedback loops inside creative development.
passiveMonitoringWaitingContextSwitching.simulatorImplication: Use briefings and Slack interruptions to create context switching, without making passive reading dominate the simulator.

workMixDesign.simulatorPlan: The simulator is a single milestone day on a family-friendly AAA console project. The player reads a build workspace, drafts one focused mechanic teaching note, turns it into a playtest watchlist, observes a small playtest session, ranks the fix priorities from observed evidence, writes an implementation spec email, prepares for and speaks in an in-person engineering handoff, then reaches the explicit assessment gate.
workMixDesign.activeSceneMixRationale: Active scenes are mostly digital design and documentation, with one Slack decision and one spoken review. This matches the source-derived profile: game design is concrete tool/artifact work, playtest interpretation, written specs, and cross-functional communication rather than generic creativity or physical labor.

storyline.gameTitle: Wind Bud Milestone: A Game Designer Day

intro.title: Welcome to Starling Grove Studios
intro.content: You are the mid-level Game Designer on Sproutbound, a fictional family-friendly AAA console adventure. Today you need to make the new Wind Bud movement mechanic easier to learn without making the milestone bigger than the team can build.
intro.imageBrief: A bright, polished AAA game studio design area with warm wood desks, concept art of cute forest guardians on the walls, a console controller, design notebooks, and a large screen showing a whimsical green platforming level. No real company logos.

morning_briefing.title: Morning Recap
morning_briefing.content: You will start by looking at a small game room. Maya will help you notice the simple problem before you choose a change.

inputArtifacts.label: Coworker recap
inputArtifacts.summary: Maya explains the Wind Bud rule, current confusion, and playtest focus.

inputArtifacts.label: Teaching Note debugging tab
inputArtifacts.summary: Debugging notes and playtest numbers are together when the player starts the design note.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Read the recap before choosing the first design-note move.
continuity: Recap facts reappear in the later app workspace and conversation prep.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: The player starts from a coworker recap, then reads the detailed debugging and statistics inside the actual design-note workspace.

morning_briefing.imageBrief: First-person view at a bright AAA game studio desk during the morning recap. A cream laptop and notebook are open, a controller sits nearby, and a wall monitor in the background shows a whimsical forest platforming level with a small wind-flower mechanic. Keep all screen text generic or unreadable; the actual recap notes and quotes are rendered by the simulator UI.

coworkerRecap.fallback: I do not want to invent extra context here. For now, hold onto the specific handoff I just gave you and keep moving.

turns.coworkerLine: The Wind Bud rule is simple: strike the bud, read the short gust cone, and enter it from the safe side so the player can lift or glide across the gap.
answerFacts: Wind Bud is activated by striking it.
It opens a short-lived gust cone.
The player needs to enter from the safe side to cross the gap.

turns.coworkerLine: Right now some players treat the bud like background, some jump the gap and fall, and some trigger the gust but run away from it.
answerFacts: Some players miss the bud entirely.
Some players jump the gap and fall.
Some players trigger the gust but move away from it.

turns.coworkerLine: So the design question is where to add cue, motion, or success feedback so a first-time player learns the rule before the coin trail distracts them.
answerFacts: The design should improve cue, motion, or success feedback.
The coin trail can distract players before they understand the Wind Bud rule.

mechanic_hook_sheet.title: Wind Bud Teaching Note
mechanic_hook_sheet.prompt: Look at the room. Pick one room part, one small change, one success sign, and one confusion sign that the 4 PM playtest can actually watch.

fields.label: Room part
fields.placeholder: Choose one room part to improve.

fields.label: Helpful change
fields.placeholder: Choose the change the playtest will check.
options: Add motion and a soft sound to the Wind Bud so players notice it before the game message.
Add moving leaves in the wind path so players know to travel with the gust.
Add a landing glow so players know where the gust should carry them.
Soften the optional coin trail until after the player learns the Wind Bud.

fields.label: How they know it worked
fields.placeholder: Choose the success sign the tester should watch.
options: Player turns toward the Wind Bud before the game message appears.
Player strikes the Wind Bud and moves with the gust instead of backing away.
Player reaches the landing platform without verbal help.
Player reaches the landing before chasing optional coins.

fields.label: Confusion sign to watch
fields.placeholder: Choose the confusion sign the tester should watch.
options: Player waits for the game message before striking the Wind Bud.
Player looks toward optional coins before learning the Wind Bud.
Player backs away from the active wind path and falls.
Player enters the gust but misses the landing platform.

visualBoard.title: Wind Bud tutorial slice
visualBoard.subtitle: Click one part of the room to improve.
visualBoard.helperText: You only need one part. The other parts can stay the same.
visualBoard.imageAlt: A simple side-view room with a start platform, Wind Bud, wind path, landing platform, and coins.

hotspots.label: Start platform
hotspots.description: A safe place where the player can stop and look.

hotspots.label: Wind Bud
hotspots.description: The object the player needs to notice and hit.

hotspots.label: Wind path
hotspots.description: The wind that carries the player across the gap.

hotspots.label: Landing platform
hotspots.description: The safe place the player should reach.

hotspots.label: Coins
hotspots.description: A reward path that can pull attention away too early.

beats.label: Teaching note
beats.helper: Choose fixed signs that the 4 PM playtest can actually show.

inputArtifacts.label: Feature Brief - Wind Bud Tutorial
inputArtifacts.summary: What should happen and where players get confused.

inputArtifacts.label: Playtest data
inputArtifacts.summary: Two quick pieces of evidence about where players get stuck while crossing.

inputArtifacts.label: Clickable Wind Bud level slice
inputArtifacts.summary: A side-view map of the start, Wind Bud, wind path, landing platform, and coins.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Pick one room part, one buildable change, and the fixed success/confusion signs the 4 PM playtest can check.

outputArtifact.label: Wind Bud teaching note
outputArtifact.format: visual annotation plus structured design note

continuity: The bounded teaching note informs the playtest watchlist and implementation handoff without asking the fixed playtest to handle unrelated open-ended inputs.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: A designer marks the playable slice so the team can see how the mechanic is taught in the room.

sourceTabs.label: Debugging Notes
sourceTabs.content:
Quick notes

What should happen: the player notices the Wind Bud, hits it, follows the wind, and reaches the next platform.

What is going wrong:
- Some players do not notice the Wind Bud.
- Some players jump across the gap before using the wind.
- Some players chase coins before learning the main move.

Player test results
These two boxes show where players get stuck while crossing the room.

Your job: fix one part of the room, not the whole level.

metrics.metric: Tries before the wind carried players across
metrics.meaning: Players needed more than four tries on average before the wind helped them cross. The path may be hard to understand.
metrics.roomNote: This points to the blue wind between the Bud and the landing platform. A helpful change could show where the player should go.

metrics.meaning: Some players got into the wind but still did not land safely. The safe landing spot may need to be easier to see.
metrics.roomNote: This points to the platform on the right. A helpful change could make success feel clear.

playtest_watchlist.title: Wind Bud Playtest Watchlist
playtest_watchlist.content: At 4 PM, Maya will watch first-time players try the Wind Bud room. Make a {{playtest watchlist}} for Maya to use during the test. It tells the tester what to watch, what counts as success, what counts as confusion, and what should not count as proof. Use actions the tester can see on screen. Do not ask the player to explain their thinking, and do not pitch a new feature.
playtest_watchlist.prompt: Fill in one Watch Item. Each box should name a visible action, such as "turns toward the Wind Bud before the game message" or "backs away from the wind and falls."

previousResponseReference.title: Your Wind Bud Teaching Note
previousResponseReference.emptyText: Submit the Teaching Note first to see it here.

fields.label: Behavior to watch
fields.placeholder: Choose the behavior Maya should look for.
options: Player turns toward the Wind Bud before the game message.
Player follows the wind path after striking the Wind Bud.
Player aims for the landing platform after entering the gust.
Player looks at coins before learning the main move.

fields.label: Success signal
options: Player strikes the Wind Bud, rides the gust, and reaches the landing without help.
Player uses the gust from the safe side after noticing the Bud.
Player reaches the landing before chasing optional coins.
Player recovers after one retry and uses the gust correctly.

fields.label: Confusion signal
options: Player waits for the game message before striking the Bud.
Player backs away from the active wind path and falls.
Player follows the coin path before learning the Wind Bud.
Player enters the gust but misses the landing.

fields.label: Do not count as proof
fields.placeholder: Choose what Maya should not over-read.
options: Do not count a late Bud strike after the game message as clean first-read success.
Do not count saying the Bud looks cute unless movement shows understanding.
Do not count reaching coins after success as a first-use failure.
Do not treat one recovery retry as proof that the first lesson was clear.

inputArtifacts.label: Wind Bud Teaching Note
inputArtifacts.whereVisible: previous structured entry and Teaching Note workspace tabs
inputArtifacts.summary: The player's proposed cue/effect and success feedback.

inputArtifacts.label: 4 PM playtest goal
inputArtifacts.summary: Observe whether first-time players read the Wind Bud without being told.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Turn the teaching note into playtest signs the team can watch.
continuity: The Run the Playtest scene asks the player to observe those same signals in tester behavior.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: Designers often turn design ideas into watchable playtest questions before judging whether a change worked.

spec_update.title: Build Spec Update
spec_update.content: Priya and Leo need a short spec update they can act on, and Owen and Emi need clear QA and amount-of-work notes. Write it as an email so the build decision can move through the team.
spec_update.prompt: Draft a 90-220 word email. Include: player problem, proposed change, exact expected behavior, acceptance criteria, risk or open question, and QA check.
spec_update.windowTitle: New Message - Wind Bud Spec Update

emailHeaders.to: Priya Raman <priya@starlinggrove.studio>; Leo Navarro <leo@starlinggrove.studio>; Owen Brooks <owen@starlinggrove.studio>; Emi Tanaka <emi@starlinggrove.studio>
emailHeaders.subject: Wind Bud clarity patch - spec update

appTabs.label: Playtest Evidence
Watchlist Focus
appTabs.content: Use your Wind Bud Playtest Watchlist, observation log, and fix-priority ranking. The spec should turn observed behavior into a change the team can build and QA checks they can run, not just describe how the room feels.

appTabs.label: Team Needs
appTabs.content: Priya needs clear behavior and edge cases. Leo needs the intended player path. Owen needs testable acceptance criteria. Emi needs risk notes and who owns each next step.

inputArtifacts.label: Playtest Evidence
inputArtifacts.summary: The player's own Wind Bud watchlist, Run the Playtest observation log, and fix-priority ranking.

inputArtifacts.label: Watchlist Focus
inputArtifacts.summary: Reminder to connect the watchlist signal to behavior the team can build and QA checks they can run.

inputArtifacts.label: Team Needs
inputArtifacts.summary: What engineering, level design, QA, and production need from the spec.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Write a spec update email the team can build from.
continuity: The spec is used as context for engineering handoff prep and the live handoff.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: Design spec emails must turn player evidence into behavior the team can build and checks the team can test.

workSurface.title: New Message - Wind Bud Spec Update

design_review_prep.title: Prepare the Engineering Handoff
design_review_prep.content: Priya read your spec email and priority ranking. Prepare for the missing build details she is most likely to ask about before she touches gameplay code.
design_review_prep.prompt: Optional: jot a prep note up to 150 words. If you write one, focus on whichever build details your specific fix still needs: player-facing behavior, edge cases, reset or tuning details, who owns which choices, or QA checks.
design_review_prep.placeholder:
Missing build detail:
Likely Priya question:
Answer / boundary:
QA or tuning note:

appTabs.label: Prep Checklist
appTabs.content:
Priya's questions are driven by your email and priority ranking. Use these checks as prep prompts, not as a script for the call.

Possible questions Priya may ask:
- What behavior is required for your chosen fix to work?
- Which edge cases or retries could break that behavior?
- What should reset, stay saved, refresh, or remain adjustable?
- What acceptance criteria would let QA check the fix?
- Which details are must-have design rules, and which can Priya choose while building?
Team need: keep the fix small, adjustable, and easy for QA to check.

inputArtifacts.label: Prep Checklist
inputArtifacts.summary: Missing build details Priya may need clarified.

inputArtifacts.label: Your Priority Note
inputArtifacts.summary: The player's own fix-priority ranking from Run the Playtest.

inputArtifacts.label: Your Spec Update Email
inputArtifacts.summary: The player's submitted Wind Bud spec update email.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Optionally jot a concise prep note for a live engineering conversation.
continuity: If written, the prep note appears inside the in-person engineering conversation.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: Designers often jot quick build-facing reminders before handing work to engineering.

design_review.title: Engineering Handoff with Priya
design_review.content: You meet Priya in the huddle room beside the playtest lab. She is ready to build the change from your email, but she needs any remaining gameplay rules and edge cases clarified before coding.
design_review.playerGoal: Clarify the missing build details in your spec update so Priya can build the fix without guessing what the design needs, what can be adjusted, and what acceptance criteria should prove the change works.
design_review.endpoint: Priya should close the conversation once she has clear answers to the most important missing build details raised by the student's email and priority ranking.
design_review.successCriteria: A strong handoff explains the player-facing goal, names relevant edge cases or tuning rules, gives criteria QA can check, and keeps the work small enough to build.
design_review.goalPrompt: Act as Priya Raman, Gameplay Engineer, in an in-person engineering handoff. Base your questions on the student's spec update email and fix-priority ranking in the meeting context. Do not ask them to re-argue playtest priority; treat that as decided. Instead, ask engineering-facing questions only where the student's own email or ranking leaves implementation gaps. Choose from topics such as player-facing behavior, edge cases, reset or persistence behavior, tuning ownership, acceptance criteria, and what is strict design intent versus implementation choice. Do not force every topic if it is already clear or irrelevant. React cooperatively but push for precision. End once the most important gaps are covered by summarizing what you will implement and one open risk if the student's answers were unclear.

typedFallback.placeholder: Explain the build rule, edge case, tuning boundary, or QA check Priya needs.
npcPrompts: That helps. Can you clarify how the gust should reset or stay tunable after a fall?
Got it. What should QA check to confirm the change worked?
That gives me enough to build the first pass. End the handoff when you're ready.

deskWorkDesign.workSurface: in-person engineering handoff with reference workspace

inputArtifacts.label: Engineering Handoff Workspace
inputArtifacts.summary: Email-driven build gaps, relevant edge cases, tuning rules, acceptance criteria, and build boundaries.

inputArtifacts.label: Your Prep Note (optional)
inputArtifacts.summary: The player's optional prep note from the previous scene, if provided.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Clarify gameplay rules the team can build and answer engineering edge-case questions.
continuity: The engineering handoff transcript is the final communication artifact before assessment.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: A mid-level designer must clarify implementation rules live with engineering, not just write broad intent.

design_review.imageBrief: First-person view in a bright AAA game studio huddle room beside a playtest lab. Priya Raman, a practical gameplay engineer in her early 30s with tied-back dark hair, sits across the table with a laptop showing unreadable code-like blocks, a controller, and compact design notes nearby. Warm professional illustration, no real company logos.

assessment_gate.title: The Day Is Complete
assessment_gate.content: The simulated design day is complete. Your Wind Bud notes, playtest priority ranking, spec update email, and engineering handoff transcript are ready for assessment.
assessment_gate.imageBrief: A calm end-of-day game studio desk with a controller, notebook, and completed design workspace on a warm cream laptop. No assessment scores visible.

grading.title: Assessment
grading.imageBrief: A calm assessment moment represented by a warm game studio desk with the completed design workspace closed, a controller, and soft studio background. No scores, charts, grading text, or report cards visible.

final_report.title: Final Report
final_report.imageBrief: A polished final wrap-up scene at a game studio desk with a laptop showing an abstract report layout with unreadable blocks, completed notes, a controller, and concept art in the background. No exact scores or readable assessment text in the image.

playtest_workspace.title: Run the Playtest
playtest_workspace.content: You are in the playtest lab watching three first-time players run the Wind Bud room after your Teaching Note. Use the watchlist you wrote earlier, but do not assume your fix worked. Watch each moment, then record only behavior you can actually observe.
playtest_workspace.prompt: Create the observation log from the playtest. For each tester, write one conclusion with evidence: what you observed, whether it proves success, confusion, or not enough proof, and what it means for your priority ranking.
playtest_workspace.priorityPrompt: Rank the fixes you wrote in the tester notes, from highest priority to lowest priority, and justify the order.
playtest_workspace.emptyWatchlistText: Submit the Watchlist first to see it here.

events.headline: Pauses on the safe start
events.description: Tester A stops on the safe starting platform instead of moving toward the Wind Bud. The pause is useful because it shows where their first read begins.

events.headline: Looks toward the optional coins
events.description: Tester A moves off the safe start and looks toward the optional coin path before using the Wind Bud. This may be a reward-placement distraction, not proof that the bud cue worked.

events.headline: Waits for the game message beside the Bud
events.description: Tester A gets close enough for the game message to appear, then pauses instead of hitting the Bud right away. That means the Bud was reached, but the cue still may not clearly say what to do.
events.fallbackLabel: Game message appears beside the bud

events.headline: Strikes the Wind Bud late
events.description: Tester A eventually strikes the Bud and the wind appears, but only after the message. Log this as late understanding, not a clean first-read success.
events.fallbackLabel: Late bud strike after game message

events.headline: Notices the Wind Bud quickly
events.description: Tester B turns toward the Wind Bud almost immediately. This is a positive recognition sign, but it does not yet prove the movement rule is clear.

events.headline: Strikes the Wind Bud quickly
events.description: Tester B strikes the bud without waiting for the game message. This supports the object cue, but the next moments show whether the gust behavior is clear.

events.headline: Runs away from the gust
events.description: When the wind turns on, Tester B backs away from the active wind path instead of moving with it toward the landing. The object was readable, but the wind path was not.

events.headline: Falls before recovering
events.description: Tester B falls after backing away from the wind path instead of riding it across. This matters more than a preference note because it blocks progress through the first lesson.

events.headline: Retries after the fall
events.description: Tester B resets and tries again. Recovery is possible, but the first attempt still shows the lesson did not fully explain how to use the gust.

events.headline: Turns toward the bud
events.description: Tester C notices the Wind Bud and turns toward it from the safe start. This is the clearest first-read success in the session.

events.headline: Uses the gust from the safe side
events.description: Tester C enters the gust from the intended safe side. This is evidence that the main rule can work for some players.

events.headline: Reaches the landing
events.description: Tester C lands on the next platform. This is a success sign for the room's main path.

events.headline: Asks whether the coin path is optional
events.description: After success, Tester C asks whether the coins on the right are optional. Because the main rule already worked, this is likely a later coin-path clarity issue, not the main priority.

observationItems.prompt: Summarize the safe-start pause, coin attention, game message beside the Bud, and late Bud strike.
Summarize the quick Bud recognition, backing away from the wind path, fall, and retry.
Summarize the successful core path and decide how much weight to give the optional coin-path question.

deskWorkDesign.workSurface: visual playtest timeline with observation log

inputArtifacts.label: Wind Bud Playtest Watchlist
inputArtifacts.summary: The player's success, confusion, and not-enough-proof rules from the prior scene.

inputArtifacts.label: Visual tester run timeline
inputArtifacts.summary: Moment-by-moment observed behavior from Tester A, Tester B, and Tester C.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Record tester behavior, separate success, confusion, and not-enough-proof moments, then rank fix priorities with reasons.

outputArtifact.label: Run the Playtest observation log
outputArtifact.format: one conclusion with evidence per tester plus fix-priority ranking

continuity: The observation log and fix-priority ranking feed the spec update and engineering handoff.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: The player experiences the playtest loop by comparing their watchlist against actual tester behavior instead of re-reading a solved problem.

playtest_workspace.imageBrief: First-person view in a game studio playtest lab. A tester sits at a console station playing a whimsical forest platforming room while the designer watches from a laptop with an observation log, controller, stopwatch, and notebook nearby. Keep all screen text generic or unreadable; the simulator renders the readable observation notes.

maya.name: Maya Chen
maya.persona: Maya is calm, precise, and deeply player-focused. She likes playful ideas, but she pushes designers to prove how a player will understand the mechanic without the designer in the room. She is warm when the student owns tradeoffs and direct when a proposal hides scope risk.
maya.voice: Measured, encouraging, and specific. She asks one pointed follow-up before making a decision.

leo.name: Leo Navarro
leo.persona: Leo is playful, quick, and visually oriented. He cares about the player's first look, the rhythm of a room, and whether a blockout teaches through movement rather than explanation. He loves charming details but respects a clean first teach.
leo.voice: Energetic, sketchbook-in-hand, occasionally joking but never vague.

priya.name: Priya Raman
priya.persona: Priya is practical, kind, and constraint-aware. She wants the smallest clear implementation that can be tuned and tested. She appreciates designers who separate desired player experience from exact engineering implementation.

emi.name: Emi Tanaka
emi.persona: Emi is organized, warm, and quietly firm about milestone scope. She is not anti-creativity; she just wants the team to make the right call for the next playable build.

owen.name: Owen Brooks
owen.persona: Owen is direct, observant, and evidence-minded. He trusts designers who read the notes carefully and turn them into testable criteria. He dislikes vague handoffs that make QA guess what changed.
owen.voice: Dry, concrete, and focused on what can be verified.

intro.welcomeBody: Step into a mid-level Game Designer role at Starling Grove Studios. You will turn playtest confusion into a clear design change the team can test for a fictional AAA console adventure.

steps.label: Read the build
steps.content: Start with the recap, debugging notes, and playtest signals before designing.

steps.label: Make the work
steps.content: Create a teaching note, a playtest watchlist, a ranked fix list, a spec update, and a handoff.

steps.label: Align the team
steps.content: Clarify build rules in an in-person or typed engineering handoff.

glossary.blockout: A rough, ugly playable version of a level used to test layout and flow before art polish.
glossary.hook: The first clear reason a player wants to try a mechanic.
glossary.affordance: A visual or audio clue that shows how an object can be used. In this simulation, the Wind Bud needs clues that tell players it can create wind.
glossary.fail-safe: A design choice that helps players recover after a mistake without getting stuck or frustrated.
glossary.acceptance criteria: Clear checks that tell the team whether a design change works. QA can use them to test the Wind Bud fix.
glossary.telemetry: Data from a playtest or game build that shows what players did, such as retries, falls, or successful landings.
glossary.milestone: A planned checkpoint in game development. By that date, the team agrees which work should be ready.
glossary.priority ranking: A ranked list of fixes, from most important to least important, based on player impact, proof, and how hard the work is.
glossary.spec: A short design plan that tells the team what to build, why it matters, and how to check whether it worked.
glossary.edge case: A less common situation that can still break the design, such as a player backing away from the wind instead of riding it across.
glossary.QA: Quality Assurance. The people who test the game and check whether a change works as intended.
glossary.AAA: A large-budget game made by a large professional studio, usually for consoles or PC.
glossary.playtest watchlist: A short list of player actions to watch during a playtest. It helps the team record what happened instead of guessing what the player was thinking.
glossary.cue: A clue in the game, such as motion, color, sound, or placement, that helps the player know what to try.
glossary.success signal: A visible action that shows the player understood the room, such as hitting the Wind Bud and reaching the next platform without help.
glossary.confusion signal: A visible action that shows the player is still unsure, such as walking past the Wind Bud or backing away from the wind and falling.

wind_bud_teaching_note.attention_effect: Add motion and a soft sound to the Wind Bud so players notice it before the game message.
wind_bud_teaching_note.success_feedback: Player turns toward the Wind Bud before the game message appears.
wind_bud_teaching_note.confusion_signal: Player waits for the game message before striking the Wind Bud.

wind_bud_playtest_watchlist.observed_behavior: Player turns toward the Wind Bud before the game message.
wind_bud_playtest_watchlist.success_signal: Player strikes the Wind Bud, rides the gust, and reaches the landing without help.
wind_bud_playtest_watchlist.failure_signal: Player backs away from the active wind path and falls.
wind_bud_playtest_watchlist.ignore_noise: Do not count a late Bud strike after the game message as clean first-read success.

playtest_observation_log.conclusion_with_evidence: Confusion signal: Tester A paused on the safe start, looked toward the coins, and waited for the game message beside the Bud before striking it. In the priority ranking, treat the first-read cue as not solved; do not count the late strike as clean success.

Mixed signal: Tester B recognized and struck the Bud quickly, but backed away from the active wind path and fell before retrying. In the priority ranking, put wind-path direction feedback before the optional coin-path fix.

Success for the core rule: Tester C turned toward the Bud, used the wind from the safe side, and reached the landing. The optional coin-path question came after success, so keep it as backlog/noise unless it affects first-use comprehension.

sample_answers.playtest_fix_priority: A strong priority ranking puts the observed wind-path direction problem first because Tester B recognized the Bud but still failed the core movement rule, then names first-read cue or coin-pull concerns above lower-impact optional coin-path feedback.
sample_answers.spec_update: A strong spec update names the observed player confusion, describes the cue or feedback change, gives expected behavior, defines acceptance criteria, and notes QA checks.
sample_answers.design_review_prep: If provided, a strong prep note states the intended player-facing behavior, names at least one edge case, defines reset or persistence behavior, and separates design intent from implementation choice.

criteria.name: Mechanic Problem Framing
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: Chooses a room part, buildable change, success sign, and confusion sign that all fit the Wind Bud playtest evidence. Score only this task; do not use later tasks, prep notes, or follow-up artifacts as evidence. 7-8: Covers the main ideas with minor gaps in evidence, specificity, priority, notes, or response quality. 4-6: Partly complete, but several important judgments, checks, explanations, records, or next actions are missing. 0-3: Misses the task goal, ignores evidence, creates unsafe or confusing promises, or chooses signs the playtest cannot observe.

criteria.name: Playtest Watchlist
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: Selects observable behavior, success, confusion, and do-not-over-read signals that the fixed tester timeline can actually show. Score only this task; do not use later tasks, prep notes, or follow-up artifacts as evidence. 7-8: Covers the main ideas with minor gaps in evidence, specificity, priority, notes, or response quality. 4-6: Partly complete, but several important judgments, checks, explanations, records, or next actions are missing. 0-3: Misses the task goal, ignores evidence, creates unsafe or confusing promises, or picks signals unrelated to the playtest evidence.

criteria.name: Playtest Observation
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: Separates observed facts from interpretation and records how player learning, failure, or fun changed during the playtest. Score only this task; do not use later tasks, prep notes, or follow-up artifacts as evidence. 7-8: Covers the main ideas with minor gaps in evidence, specificity, priority, notes, or response quality. 4-6: Partly complete, but several important judgments, checks, explanations, records, or next actions are missing. 0-3: Misses the task goal, ignores evidence, creates unsafe or confusing promises, or is too incomplete to use.

criteria.name: Spec Update
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: Uses playtest evidence to clearly describe the spec change, kept behavior, implementation notes, open questions, and QA checks. Score only this task; do not use later tasks, prep notes, or follow-up artifacts as evidence. 7-8: Covers the main ideas with minor gaps in evidence, specificity, priority, notes, or response quality. 4-6: Partly complete, but several important judgments, checks, explanations, records, or next actions are missing. 0-3: Misses the task goal, ignores evidence, creates unsafe or confusing promises, or is too incomplete to use.

criteria.name: Engineering Handoff
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: In the actual handoff transcript, not the prep note alone, explains design intent, why the spec changed, implementation constraints, and open decisions to engineering. Score only this task; do not use later tasks, prep notes, or follow-up artifacts as evidence. 7-8: Covers the main ideas with minor gaps in evidence, specificity, priority, notes, or response quality. 4-6: Partly complete, but several important judgments, checks, explanations, records, or next actions are missing. 0-3: Misses the task goal, ignores evidence, creates unsafe or confusing promises, or is too incomplete to use.
